text: One child, one teacher, one book, one pen can change the world.
speech: Address to the United Nations Youth Assembly
context: Delivered on her 16th birthday, months after surviving an assassination attempt by the Taliban.

text: They thought that the bullets would silence us, but they failed. And out of that silence came thousands of voices. The terrorists thought that they would change my aims and stop my ambitions. But nothing changed in my life except this: weakness, fear and hopelessness died. Strength, power and courage were born.
context: The powerful core of her first major international address after the attack.

text: I speak not for myself, but so those without a voice can be heard. Those who have fought for their rights. Their right to live in peace. Their right to be treated with dignity. Their right to equality of opportunity. Their right to be educated.
context: Speaking on behalf of girls denied education worldwide.

text: Let us pick up our books and our pens. They are our most powerful weapons. One child, one teacher, one book and one pen can change the world.
context: The closing call to action of the UN speech.

text: I don't want to be remembered as the girl who was shot. I want to be remembered as the girl who stood up.
context: Reframing her identity around courage rather than victimhood.

text: When the whole world is silent, even one voice becomes powerful.
source: Various addresses and her memoir I Am Malala
context: The power of a single voice against silence and oppression.

text: We realize the importance of our voices only when we are silenced.
context: Reflecting on the value of speech after the attempt to silence her.

text: Education is neither Eastern nor Western. Education is education. The only difference is in the methods used to provide it.
context: Rejecting cultural relativism as an excuse to deny girls schooling.

text: I raise up my voice — not so I can shout, but so that those without a voice can be heard. We cannot succeed when half of us are held back.
context: Linking her voice to the millions of silenced girls.

text: Let us remember: One book, one pen, one child, and one teacher can change the world.
context: Slight variation emphasizing the teacher.

text: I am Malala. And I am here to stand up for the rights of girls to go to school everywhere in the world.
context: Simple, powerful self-identification and mission statement.

text: Peace in every home, every street, every village, every country — this is my dream. Education for every boy and every girl in the world. To sit down on a chair and read my books with all my friends at school is my right.
speech: Nobel Peace Prize Acceptance and Lecture
context: Her Nobel lecture in Oslo, the youngest recipient ever.

text: I tell my story, not because it is unique, but because it is not. It is the story of many girls.
context: Insisting that her experience represents millions of others.

text: We call upon our sisters around the world to be brave — to embrace the strength within themselves and realize their full potential.
context: Direct appeal to girls and women globally.

text: Dear sisters and brothers, we realize the importance of light when we see darkness. We realize the importance of our voice when we are silenced.
context: Opening of the Nobel lecture, echoing her UN speech.

text: Let us pick up our books and pens. They are our most powerful weapons. One child, one teacher, one book, one pen can change the world.
context: Repeating the famous line in her Nobel address.

text: I am those 66 million girls who are deprived of education. And today I am not raising my voice, it is the voice of those 66 million girls.
context: Speaking as a representative of all out-of-school girls.

text: The extremists are afraid of books and pens. The power of education frightens them.
context: Explaining why the Taliban targeted her and other girls' schools.

text: I do not even hate the Talib who shot me. Even if there is a gun in my hand and he stands in front of me, I would not shoot him.
context: Demonstrating the depth of her commitment to non-violence and forgiveness.

text: With guns you can kill terrorists, with education you can kill terrorism.
speech: Multiple speeches on education vs. violence
context: The strategic and moral argument for education as the long-term solution.
